questions.question: Which Hinchey stage describes a paracolic abscess?
questions.explanation: The Hinchey classification for acute diverticulitis divides the stages according to the extent of the infection: Hinchey I (confined pericolic abscess), Hinchey II (pelvic or paracolic abscess), Hinchey III (purulent peritonitis) and Hinchey IV (fecaloid peritonitis).

questions.question: What is the surgical treatment of choice in Hinchey III?
questions.explanation: In Hinchey III (purulent peritonitis), laparoscopic lavage plus drainage has gained acceptance as a minimally invasive surgical option, reserving resection for cases without improvement or with fecaloid peritonitis (Hinchey IV).

questions.question: What tomographic finding characterizes Hinchey stage I?
questions.explanation: In Hinchey I, a small pericolic abscess (less than 4 cm) confined to the mesentery or adjacent to the colon is observed; Major or distant abscesses correspond to Hinchey II, and peritonitis to stages III or IV.

questions.question: What is the recommended initial management in Hinchey I?
questions.explanation: In Hinchey I (small confined abscess) conservative management with oral or intravenous antibiotics and observation is sufficient; Surgery or percutaneous drainage is reserved for larger abscesses or failure of medical treatment.

questions.question: What complication defines Hinchey stage IV?
questions.explanation: Hinchey IV is characterized by diffuse fecaloid peritonitis due to free perforation of the colon, requiring urgent surgery with resection and lavage; Abscesses correspond to stages I–II and fistulas are chronic complications not classified by Hinchey.

questions.question: What is the treatment of choice in Hinchey II with an abscess greater than 5 cm?
questions.explanation: In Hinchey II, abscesses larger than 4–5 cm are managed with CT-guided percutaneous drainage to control the septic source, followed by antibiotics; Surgery is reserved for drainage failure or patient instability.

questions.question: What tomographic finding characterizes the chord sign in acute diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: The chord sign on CT corresponds to the linear densification of mesenteric fat adjacent to the inflamed colon, reflecting the extension of inflammation along the mesenteric vessels.

questions.question: What is the most common location of the chord sign in diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Acute diverticulitis most commonly affects the sigmoid colon, and the chord sign is typically seen in the mesenteric fat of this region due to peridiverticular inflammation.

questions.question: What anatomical structure is affected in the chord sign on CT?
questions.explanation: The rope sign describes inflammation of the mesenteric fat adjacent to the affected colon, appearing as linear densification or striae on CT, without direct involvement of the muscle wall or lumen.

questions.question: What is the main differential diagnosis of the chord sign on CT?
questions.explanation: Acute appendicitis may present a similar chord sign in the mesenteric fat of the right lower quadrant, making it the main differential diagnosis, especially when diverticulitis affects the right colon.

questions.question: What additional finding usually accompanies the chord sign in acute diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: In acute diverticulitis, the rope sign is frequently associated with localized mural thickening of the affected colon, visible on CT as a segmental thickening of the colonic wall.

questions.question: In which phase of acute diverticulitis is the chord sign most evident on CT?
questions.explanation: The chord sign is most prominent during the acute inflammatory phase of diverticulitis, when inflammation of the mesenteric fat is maximal and is seen as linear densification on CT.

questions.question: What is the main clinical sign that differentiates right diverticulitis from acute appendicitis?
questions.explanation: Right diverticulitis usually presents pain in the left or diffuse iliac fossa, while acute appendicitis typically presents pain in the right iliac fossa.

questions.question: Which imaging study is most useful to differentiate right diverticulitis from acute appendicitis?
questions.explanation: Contrast-enhanced computed tomography has high sensitivity and specificity in distinguishing right diverticulitis from acute appendicitis, showing mural thickening and diverticula.

questions.question: Which tomographic finding is characteristic of right diverticulitis rather than appendicitis?
questions.explanation: Thickening of the cecal wall with inflamed diverticula is typical of right diverticulitis, while a dilated appendix and appendicolith are seen in appendicitis.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for uncomplicated right diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Uncomplicated right diverticulitis is managed with broad-spectrum antibiotics and bowel rest, similar to left diverticulitis, without initial surgery.

questions.question: What complication is more common in right diverticulitis than in acute appendicitis?
questions.explanation: Enterocutaneous fistula is a more common complication of recurrent right diverticulitis, while perforated appendicitis usually causes localized peritonitis.

questions.question: What epidemiological factor is more associated with right diverticulitis than with appendicitis?
questions.explanation: A low-fiber diet is a risk factor for diverticulosis and right diverticulitis, while appendicitis is more common in young people with no clear dietary relationship.

questions.question: What is the Hinchey stage indicating pericolic abscess?
questions.explanation: In Hinchey's classification, stage II is characterized by the presence of a pericolic or pelvic abscess, unlike stage I which only shows phlegmon or an abscess confined to the wall.

questions.question: Which tomographic finding corresponds to Hinchey III?
questions.explanation: Hinchey III describes diffuse purulent peritonitis without evident free perforation, while Hinchey IV involves fecal peritonitis due to open perforation. The others are minor stages.

questions.question: What is the indicated surgical management for Hinchey IV?
questions.explanation: In Hinchey IV (fecaloid peritonitis), resection of the affected segment with end colostomy (Hartmann) is preferred due to the high risk of anastomotic leak. The other options are not safe in this context.

questions.question: What criterion defines Hinchey I in acute diverticulitis?
options: Abscess confined to the colonic wall
questions.explanation: Hinchey I is defined as an abscess or phlegmon confined to the wall of the colon, without significant extension to the peritoneal cavity. Stage II involves pericolic or pelvic abscess.

questions.question: What is the main difference between Hinchey III and Hinchey IV?
questions.explanation: The key difference is the type of peritonitis: Hinchey III presents with diffuse purulent peritonitis, while Hinchey IV shows fecaloid peritonitis due to free perforation. This determines a more aggressive surgical management.

questions.question: What CT finding contraindicates initial conservative management?
questions.explanation: Diffuse fecaloid peritonitis (Hinchey IV) requires emergency surgery, not conservative management. The other options can be managed initially with antibiotics or percutaneous drainage.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment in acute uncomplicated diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: In uncomplicated acute diverticulitis, initial medical treatment consists of oral antibiotics (such as metronidazole plus ciprofloxacin) and liquid diet, reserving surgery for complicated cases or therapeutic failure.

questions.question: Which tomographic finding indicates diverticulitis complicated with abscess?
questions.explanation: The presence of a fluid collection (abscess) on abdominal CT is a criterion for complicated diverticulitis, which often requires percutaneous drainage or surgery, unlike the wall thickening typical of the uncomplicated form.

questions.question: When is elective surgery indicated for acute diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Elective surgery is indicated in diverticulitis complicated with fistula (colovesical, colovaginal), stenosis or recurrent abscess, not in single uncomplicated episodes or in small abscesses manageable with drainage.

questions.question: What clinical criterion suggests failure of medical treatment in diverticulitis?
options: Mild pain in the iliac fossa
questions.explanation: Persistent fever (>48 hours) despite antibiotics is a sign of therapeutic failure that requires considering percutaneous drainage or surgery, while mild leukocytosis and controlled pain are expected in a favorable outcome.

questions.question: What type of surgery is preferred in perforated diverticulitis with peritonitis?
questions.explanation: In diffuse peritonitis due to perforated diverticulitis, Hartmann surgery (sigmoid colectomy with colostomy) is the procedure of choice, as it avoids anastomoses in a contaminated abdomen, reducing the risk of dehiscence.

questions.question: Which antibiotic is associated with a higher risk of Clostridium difficile colitis in diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Clindamycin has a high risk of inducing Clostridium difficile colitis, so it is not recommended as first line in diverticulitis; Combinations such as metronidazole plus ciprofloxacin or amoxicillin-clavulanic acid are preferred.

questions.question: What is the main indication for urgent surgery in acute diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Generalized peritonitis indicates free perforation with diffuse contamination, requiring urgent surgery to control the septic focus.

questions.question: What tomographic finding requires urgent surgery in diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Intra-abdominal free air is a sign of free perforation, an urgent surgical indication due to the risk of fecaloid peritonitis.

questions.question: What clinical criterion indicates failure of medical treatment in diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: The persistence of fever after 72 hours of antibiotics suggests failure of conservative treatment, indicating urgent surgery.

questions.question: Which complication of diverticulitis requires emergency surgery?
questions.explanation: Complete intestinal obstruction due to diverticulitis does not resolve with conservative measures, requiring urgent surgery for decompression.

questions.question: What physical sign is an absolute indication for urgent surgery?
questions.explanation: Generalized abdominal guarding indicates diffuse peritoneal irritation, a sign of peritonitis requiring urgent laparotomy.

questions.question: What condition in diverticulitis contraindicates conservative management?
questions.explanation: Sepsis of abdominal origin indicates organ failure due to uncontrolled infection, requiring urgent surgery for drainage and resection.

questions.question: What is the antibiotic of choice in uncomplicated diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is the first-line antibiotic in uncomplicated diverticulitis due to its coverage against gram-negative and anaerobes.

questions.question: When is antibiotic therapy indicated in uncomplicated diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: In uncomplicated diverticulitis, antibiotic therapy is reserved for patients with comorbidities or immunosuppression, since the majority is managed with observation.

questions.question: What does the antibiotic regimen cover in uncomplicated diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: The flora involved in diverticulitis includes gram-negative and anaerobic bacilli, so antibiotic therapy must cover both groups.

questions.question: What is an alternative to amoxicillin-clavulanate in uncomplicated diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Ciprofloxacin plus metronidazole covers gram-negative and anaerobic infections, being an alternative when there is an allergy or intolerance to beta-lactams.

questions.question: What is the recommended duration of antibiotics in uncomplicated diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: The recommended duration is 3 to 5 days, based on evidence that short courses are equally effective and reduce resistance.

questions.question: What factor contraindicates management without antibiotics in diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Immunosuppression is a risk factor for poor outcome, so these patients require antibiotic therapy even in uncomplicated diverticulitis.

questions.question: In the outpatient management of acute uncomplicated diverticulitis, what type of diet is recommended initially?
questions.explanation: A clear liquid diet is recommended for the first 24-48 hours to allow intestinal rest and reduce inflammation in acute uncomplicated diverticulitis managed on an outpatient basis.

questions.question: What is the main objective of clear liquid diet in acute outpatient diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Colon rest is the main objective of the clear liquid diet, since it reduces intraluminal pressure and inflammation, favoring the resolution of the infectious process in acute diverticulitis.

questions.question: Which oral antibiotic is first-line for the outpatient management of acute uncomplicated diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Ciprofloxacin, combined with metronidazole, is one of the first-line oral antibiotic regimens to cover gram-negative bacteria and anaerobes in uncomplicated acute diverticulitis managed on an outpatient basis.

questions.question: What is the most important clinical criterion to decide outpatient management in acute diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: The absence of signs of peritonitis or complications (abscess, perforation) is the key criterion for safe outpatient management, along with oral tolerance and good social support.

questions.question: What dietary progression is recommended after 48 hours of improvement in acute outpatient diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: A soft, low-fiber diet is recommended after clinical improvement to avoid colonic distension while the inflammatory process resolves; fiber is reintroduced weeks later.

questions.question: What clinical sign contraindicates outpatient management of acute diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Voluntary abdominal guarding suggests peritoneal irritation, indicating complicated disease or perforation, requiring hospitalization and surgical or intravenous management.

questions.question: What is the most characteristic tomographic finding of peridiverticular abscess?
questions.explanation: Peridiverticular abscess presents as a liquid collection with gas on CT, secondary to contained perforation of an inflamed diverticulum.

questions.question: What is the treatment of choice for a peridiverticular abscess smaller than 3 cm?
questions.explanation: Peridiverticular abscesses smaller than 3 cm usually respond to broad-spectrum antibiotics, reserving percutaneous drainage for larger collections.

questions.question: What bacteria is most frequently isolated in peridiverticular abscesses?
questions.explanation: Escherichia coli is the most common germ in peridiverticular abscesses, due to the usual colonic flora involved in diverticular perforation.

questions.question: What is the Hinchey classification for peridiverticular abscess?
questions.explanation: Hinchey's classification divides complicated diverticulitis into stages I to IV; Peridiverticular abscess corresponds to stage I (pericolic abscess).

questions.question: What clinical sign suggests a complicated peridiverticular abscess?
questions.explanation: Fever associated with a palpable mass in the left lower quadrant is characteristic of a peridiverticular abscess, indicating a localized inflammatory collection.

questions.question: What is the most feared complication of an undrained peridiverticular abscess?
questions.explanation: Generalized peritonitis is the most serious complication, since rupture of the abscess into the peritoneal cavity spreads the infection and requires emergency surgery.

questions.question: What is the emergency surgical indication in complicated acute diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Free perforation with generalized peritonitis requires urgent surgery due to the risk of sepsis and organ failure. The other options can be managed conservatively or electively.

questions.question: What tomographic finding is characteristic of free perforation in diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Extraluminal free air on CT indicates perforation of the hollow viscus. The thickening and abscess are compatible with diverticulitis without free perforation; the stenosis is chronic.

questions.question: What is the most frequently isolated germ in peritonitis secondary to perforated diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Escherichia coli is the most common facultative anaerobic gram-negative bacillus in intra-abdominal infections of colonic origin. The other pathogens are less prevalent in this context.

questions.question: Which clinical sign is most specific for generalized peritonitis?
questions.explanation: Involuntary abdominal contracture (muscle defense) indicates diffuse peritoneal irritation. Localized pain is typical of nonperforated processes; distension and fever are nonspecific.

questions.question: What is the recommended initial management in a patient with peritonitis due to perforated diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Peritonitis due to free perforation requires urgent surgery plus broad-spectrum antibiotics. Percutaneous drainage is for localized abscesses; colonoscopy is contraindicated in perforation.

questions.question: What surgical procedure is preferred in fecaloid peritonitis due to diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Hartmann surgery (resection of the affected segment and terminal colostomy) is the standard in severe peritonitis with fecal contamination. The primary anastomosis has a high risk of dehiscence.

questions.question: What is the main risk factor for recurrence of acute diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Advanced age is associated with a higher risk of recurrence due to structural changes in the colonic wall and a lower capacity for tissue repair.

questions.question: What clinical criterion indicates the need for surgical prophylaxis after an episode of diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: A single complicated episode (abscess, perforation) has a high risk of severe recurrence, so elective prophylactic surgery is recommended.

questions.question: What is the absolute surgical indication in acute recurrent diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Generalized peritonitis due to diverticular perforation is an absolute surgical emergency, since it involves peritoneal contamination and risk of sepsis.

questions.question: Which surgical procedure is preferred in the prophylaxis of diverticulitis recurrence?
questions.explanation: Sigmoid resection is the surgery of choice because most diverticula are located in the sigmoid colon, eliminating the affected segment.

questions.question: Which tomographic finding contraindicates immediate elective surgical prophylaxis?
questions.explanation: Pneumatosis intestinalis indicates ischemia or necrosis of the colonic wall, requiring emergency surgery rather than elective prophylaxis.

questions.question: What is the approximate recurrence rate after a first episode of acute diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: After a first episode, recurrence is reported in 15-20% of patients, being more frequent in those with risk factors such as immunosuppression.

questions.question: What is the most suggestive endoscopic finding of colorectal cancer in a patient with suspected acute diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: An ulcerated, friable mass is characteristic of colorectal cancer, while findings of diverticulitis include erythematous mucosa, diverticula with exudate, and thickened folds.

questions.question: Which symptom is more characteristic of colorectal cancer than of acute diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Unexplained weight loss is a constitutional symptom most associated with advanced colorectal cancer, while pain, fever, and acute intestinal changes are common in diverticulitis.

questions.question: Which imaging test is most useful to differentiate acute diverticulitis from colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Contrast-enhanced computed tomography is the test of choice to differentiate both entities, showing segmental mural thickening in diverticulitis and heterogeneous mass in cancer.

questions.question: Which tomographic finding is more indicative of colorectal cancer than diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Short, asymmetrical mural thickening is typical of colorectal cancer, while long concentric thickening and fatty infiltration are more typical of diverticulitis.

questions.question: What tumor marker can be elevated in colorectal cancer and rarely in diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is a tumor marker that is elevated in colorectal cancer, while CRP, ESR and leukocytes are nonspecific acute phase reactants also elevated in diverticulitis.

questions.question: What is the main indication for colonoscopy after an episode of acute diverticulitis?
questions.explanation: Colonoscopy is recommended after an episode of acute diverticulitis to rule out colorectal cancer, since both entities can share initial symptoms and radiological findings.
